title: Srinivasa Ramanujan
summary: The poor, almost entirely self-taught South Indian clerk who, with little formal training, produced mathematics of such strange depth that it still feels less discovered than received — who sent his theorems to Cambridge, worked with G.H. Hardy, was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society, and died at 32, the great modern emblem of raw, untaught mathematical genius.
hook: A young clerk in South India, with almost no formal training, filled notebooks with thousands of mathematical formulas no one had taught him. In 1913 he mailed some to a famous Cambridge professor. The professor decided they had to be true, because no one would have the imagination to invent them. So he brought the clerk across the world. Within five years Srinivasa Ramanujan was a Fellow of the Royal Society. Within seven he was dead, at 32. He said a goddess showed him the formulas in his sleep.

beginner.theFigure:
In a small town in South India, a young man sat with a slate and a stack of cheap paper. He had no university degree. He had failed most of his college subjects. He worked as a clerk in a shipping office in Madras, counting and recording numbers all day. In his free hours he did something stranger. He filled notebook after notebook with mathematics that no one had taught him.

These were not school sums. They were deep results about numbers, written in his own private style, page after page. He found formulas that trained mathematicians had never seen. Some were so unusual that, decades later, experts were still proving him right.

His name was Srinivasa Ramanujan. He was born in 1887 and died in 1920, at only 32. He had little money and little formal training. Yet he produced mathematics of such depth that people still describe it with a kind of awe. He himself said the ideas were not really his. He believed a goddess revealed them to him while he slept.
beginner.worldOfIdeas:
Ramanujan came from a poor family in Tamil Nadu, in the south of India. As a boy he was good at school, but only because school was easy. The thing that gripped him was mathematics, and nothing else.

When he was about sixteen, he got hold of a cheap book. It listed thousands of mathematical results, one after another, with almost no explanation. For most students this would have been dull. For Ramanujan it was a door. He worked through the results and then kept going on his own, far past the book.

The problem was that he cared about nothing else. He stopped studying his other subjects. He failed his exams and lost his college scholarship, more than once. To his family he looked like a young man throwing his life away. He married, needed an income, and took the clerk's job at the Madras Port Trust. Mathematics stayed his real work, done on the side, in the margins of an ordinary life.
beginner.centralIdea:
What made Ramanujan special is hard to put into words, but here is the heart of it. Most mathematicians build a result step by step, slowly, checking each move. Ramanujan seemed to skip the steps. A finished formula would simply arrive in his mind, often strange and exact.

He was unusually close to numbers. There is a famous story about this. He was ill in a hospital in England. A visitor mentioned he had arrived in a taxi numbered 1729, and called it a dull number. Ramanujan answered at once. It was not dull at all. It was the smallest number you can write as two cubes added together in two different ways.

Most people would need pen, paper, and time to check such a thing. He knew it instantly, the way you might know the face of a friend. The whole world of whole numbers seemed familiar to him, as if he had lived among them.
beginner.howSpread:
In 1913, Ramanujan did something bold. He wrote a letter to G.H. Hardy, one of the leading mathematicians in England, at the University of Cambridge. The letter was packed with results, most of them unproven, many of them astonishing.

Hardy almost set it aside. Strangers sent him strange letters. But these formulas would not leave him alone. Some he recognized as true and very hard. Others he had never seen. He judged that they were too deep to be a clever fake. He arranged to bring Ramanujan to Cambridge.

In 1914 Ramanujan sailed to England. There he and Hardy worked together for five years. It was an odd pair. Hardy wanted careful proof for everything. Ramanujan trusted his sudden insight. Yet together they produced beautiful results. In 1918 the Royal Society, Britain's leading body of scientists, made him a member, a rare honor and one of the first given to an Indian.
beginner.contestedInheritance:
Ramanujan's time in England was brilliant and brief. The cold climate did not suit him. The First World War made food scarce, and as a strict vegetarian he struggled to eat well. He fell seriously ill. In 1919 he sailed home to India, and in 1920 he died, at 32. The exact cause is still debated.

He left behind notebooks crammed with results. Many had no proofs, just the answers. Mathematicians have spent a hundred years catching up with them. In 1976 a forgotten set of his pages, now called the 'lost notebook,' was rediscovered. It still feeds new research today.

There is also the matter of the goddess. Ramanujan was a devout Hindu. He said the formulas came from Namagiri, his family's goddess, in his dreams. People understand this in different ways. Some take it as a sincere account of how creation felt to him. Others see a religious frame around an extraordinary natural gift. What is not in doubt is the mathematics he left.

general.theFigure:
What Ramanujan made possible was a body of mathematics that the discipline is still mining, produced by a man almost entirely outside its training system. Born in 1887 in colonial South India, raised in poverty, he had no advanced schooling and failed the college courses that were not mathematics. He found his results by a route few others have traveled, and a century of work has confirmed how deep many of them go.

His story turns on a single letter. In 1913 he wrote to the Cambridge mathematician G.H. Hardy, enclosing pages of unproven theorems. Hardy judged that no one could have invented them, brought him to England in 1914, and worked with him until 1919. In 1918 he was elected to the Royal Society, among the first Indians so honored, and to a fellowship at Trinity College. He returned to India in 1919 and died the next year, at 32.
general.worldOfIdeas:
Ramanujan's mathematical world began with a single book. Around 1903 he obtained G.S. Carr's 'Synopsis of Elementary Results,' a dense list of thousands of theorems with little proof. He absorbed it and then pushed far beyond it, working in isolation with no teacher to guide or correct him.

This isolation shaped him. He developed his own notation and his own habits of thought, and he often did not distinguish sharply between a result he had proven and one he simply trusted. Cut off from the wider field, he sometimes rediscovered known theorems, unaware they existed. More often he found things no one had seen. His early life was governed by one fact: he would do mathematics and almost nothing else, even when it cost him scholarships, exams, and a settled career.

To survive he took a clerk's post at the Madras Port Trust in 1912. His superiors, and a few local mathematicians, came to see that the quiet accountant filling notebooks was doing something far beyond routine. That recognition, slow and partial, is what eventually pushed his work toward Cambridge.
general.centralIdea:
Ramanujan's gift is usually described as intuition, but the word undersells it. He produced exact identities, infinite series, and continued fractions that seem to arrive whole, without the visible scaffolding of proof. Some are formulas for pi that converge with startling speed. Others concern partitions, the number of ways to break a whole number into a sum of smaller whole numbers.

With Hardy, he developed what became known as the circle method and an asymptotic formula that estimates the partition count for large numbers with remarkable accuracy. He worked on highly composite numbers, on the function now called the Ramanujan tau function, and, near the end of his life, on mysterious objects he called mock theta functions, whose full meaning emerged only decades later.

The taxicab story captures the man. Told that the cab number 1729 seemed dull, he replied that it was the smallest number expressible as a sum of two cubes in two different ways. The anecdote, recorded by Hardy, is not really about a trick. It points to an unusual intimacy with the integers, a sense that each number had a particular character he already knew.
general.howSpread:
The collaboration with Hardy is one of the famous partnerships in the history of science, and an unlikely one. Hardy was a rigorous English don who held proof as the heart of mathematics. Ramanujan trusted insight and often skipped the demonstration. Each found the other strange. Hardy later said his greatest contribution to mathematics was discovering Ramanujan.

Their joint work between 1914 and 1919, especially on partitions, gave the field tools it still uses. The asymptotic partition formula and the circle method opened a whole area of analytic number theory. Hardy also faced the harder task of teaching a self-taught genius the proofs he had skipped, without breaking the spring of his originality.

Much of Ramanujan's wider influence came after his death, through his notebooks. The results he recorded without proof became a research program for others. Bruce Berndt spent decades editing and proving the notebook entries. The mock theta functions, once a puzzle, were connected in the 2000s to a broader theory and even to questions in physics. His reach grew long after he was gone.
general.contestedInheritance:
Ramanujan's death at 32 is the central tragedy of his story, and its cause is genuinely uncertain. For most of the twentieth century it was attributed to tuberculosis. A later medical reassessment argued instead for hepatic amoebiasis, a treatable parasitic infection of the liver. The evidence is thin, and the question remains open. What is clear is that wartime England, a cold climate, illness, and the difficulty of keeping a strict vegetarian diet wore down his health.

The deeper debate concerns how to understand his methods. Some see a unique, almost inexplicable intuition. Others, including later mathematicians who studied the notebooks, argue that his leaps, though astonishing, followed patterns of analogy and pattern-recognition that can be partly reconstructed. The 'untaught genius' image is true, but it can flatten a thinker who was, in his own way, systematic.

There is also the question of faith. Ramanujan, a devout Hindu, credited his insights to the goddess Namagiri of Namakkal, saying she revealed formulas in dreams. This is best presented as his own sincere account of his experience. It is neither a claim to be proven nor a superstition to be mocked, but part of how one of the modern world's great minds understood his own gift.

advanced.theFigure: The historical reconstruction of Ramanujan rests on an unusual archive: notebooks dense with results and sparse with proofs, a handful of letters, and Hardy's own recollections, which are vivid but shaped by Hardy's purposes. The image of the isolated, untaught genius is partly a product of these sources and of Hardy's framing of them. Recovering the actual texture of Ramanujan's mathematical development means working against the grain of an account that had reasons to emphasize his strangeness and his distance from the European tradition.
advanced.worldOfIdeas:
The interpretive problem at the center of Ramanujan studies is how to characterize a mathematical mind that did not separate conjecture from theorem in the conventional way. Hardy estimated that roughly a third of the early notebook claims were already known, a third were new and provable, and a third were either false or beyond reach. That distribution is itself the puzzle: a thinker capable of formulas of extraordinary depth was also capable of confidently asserting results that did not hold.

The later editorial project, principally Bruce Berndt's multivolume proof of the notebook entries, complicated the romance of pure intuition. Berndt's work showed that many of Ramanujan's leaps, when reconstructed, rested on identifiable techniques of transformation and analogy, even where Ramanujan left no trace of method. This neither dissolves the originality nor fully explains it. It relocates the mystery from the existence of the results to the cognitive process that found them without the usual apparatus.
advanced.centralIdea:
The mock theta functions are the sharpest case of Ramanujan's reach exceeding his era's vocabulary. Introduced in his final letter to Hardy in 1920, they were defined by examples rather than by a general theory, and for decades no one could say precisely what class of objects they belonged to. The resolution came only in the 2000s, when Sander Zwegers showed they are holomorphic parts of harmonic Maass forms, embedding them in a structure unavailable in Ramanujan's lifetime.

This is the genuinely uncomfortable point. A man working largely alone, with a single synopsis for training, anticipated mathematical objects whose proper home required eighty years of subsequent development to construct. The tau function and the congruences he observed for partition numbers fed similarly into modular forms theory and, downstream, into the machinery used in the proof of results far beyond his own time. The standard account treats him as a precursor; the harder claim is that his examples encoded structure his contemporaries had no means to recognize.
advanced.howSpread:
The Hardy collaboration raises a question that resists sentiment: what did rigor cost, and what did it save? Hardy's insistence on proof disciplined results that Ramanujan would otherwise have left as assertions, making them transmissible. But Hardy himself worried that formal training might have damaged the originality, that the very gaps in Ramanujan's education were conditions of his strangeness. The counterfactual is unanswerable and has become a fixture of the discussion: whether a Ramanujan put through a standard European mathematical curriculum would have produced more, or less, or something tamer.

The colonial dimension is inseparable from this. The gulf Ramanujan crossed was not only intellectual but material and racial, a passage from a Madras shipping office to Trinity College that the imperial structure both obstructed and, through Cambridge, made possible. His recognition by the Royal Society in 1918 was real, and it was also legible within an imperial narrative about the empire's capacity to discover and elevate native talent. Both readings hold at once.
advanced.contestedInheritance:
The contested inheritance is ultimately about what kind of object Ramanujan's intuition was. The deflationary reading treats the goddess attribution and the dream-revelation accounts as a cultural idiom layered over an exceptional but natural pattern-recognition faculty. The opposing temptation, to treat his process as inexplicable, is a failure of analysis dressed as reverence. Neither extreme survives close contact with the notebooks, which show both uncanny reach and ordinary error.

The medical question about his death, tuberculosis versus hepatic amoebiasis, is itself a small case of the larger evidentiary problem: the record is too thin to settle, and confident verdicts in either direction outrun the documents. What endures is not a resolved figure but a productive one. The 'lost notebook,' recovered in 1976, continues to generate theorems and connections its author never stated, which is perhaps the strangest measure of the work: that it still answers questions no one had yet thought to ask of it.

foundation.theStory: Ramanujan is a case study in what mathematical genius is, and how knowledge crosses a vast gulf of distance, money, and culture. A self-taught clerk in colonial South India produced advanced results with almost no formal training, sent them to Cambridge, and was recognized there before dying young. His notebooks, full of unproven formulas, became a research program that outlived him by a century. His own explanation of his gift was religious: he credited the goddess Namagiri.
foundation.theDebate: The debates are several. How should we characterize his method, given that he did not separate conjecture from proof in the usual way? How much does the 'untaught genius' image distort a thinker who was, in his own fashion, systematic? What caused his early death, long blamed on tuberculosis but later reassessed? And how should we read his religious account of his own insight without either endorsing or mocking it?
foundation.whyItMatters: Ramanujan's case opens questions that reach well beyond mathematics: how training both enables and constrains originality, how the colonial relationship shaped whose talent got seen and on what terms, and how we credit insight whose source the thinker himself described in religious language. It is also a way into the history of number theory, the partition problem, and the long afterlife of a body of work whose author left few proofs.
questions: Ramanujan had almost no formal training, yet produced deep results. What does his case suggest about the relationship between education and mathematical originality?
He credited his formulas to the goddess Namagiri, revealed in dreams. How should a historical account treat a thinker's own explanation of his gift when that explanation is religious?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The essential life is Robert Kanigel's 'The Man Who Knew Infinity,' which set the modern popular and much of the scholarly framing. For the mathematics, Bruce Berndt's multivolume edition proving the notebook entries is the foundational technical project, and George Andrews's recovery and study of the 'lost notebook' reshaped the field. Hardy's own writings, including 'A Mathematician's Apology' and his memoir of Ramanujan, are primary but partial, shaped by Hardy's perspective.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The notebooks are an unusually rich primary record of results, but a poor record of method, since Ramanujan rarely wrote his reasoning down. Hardy's recollections are vivid and central but reflect one vantage. Biographical detail about his inner life and his religious experience comes largely through later report. The cause of death cannot be established from the surviving medical record with confidence.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong argument about Ramanujan has to resist two easy moves. The first is pure mystification, treating his intuition as beyond analysis, which Berndt's reconstructions show to be unearned. The second is full deflation, explaining the genius entirely as ordinary pattern-recognition, which the mock theta functions, anticipating structure by eighty years, will not support. A defensible position holds both: that his process was partly reconstructible and still left a genuine residue that reconstruction does not dissolve. On the death question, the honest position is that the evidence underdetermines the verdict, and that confident claims of either tuberculosis or amoebiasis exceed the documents.
questions: Berndt's edition reconstructs proofs for results Ramanujan stated without them. Does successful reconstruction explain his method, or only confirm his conclusions?
The mock theta functions found their proper theory eighty years later. What does it mean to say a thinker 'anticipated' mathematics that did not yet exist?

research.topic: Ramanujan's mathematical results and their durability
research.content: Ramanujan's contributions to partitions (including the asymptotic formula and circle method developed with Hardy), highly composite numbers, the tau function, infinite series for pi, continued fractions, and mock theta functions are established and central to analytic number theory and related fields. His election as Fellow of the Royal Society in 1918 and Fellow of Trinity College is documented. Bruce Berndt's multivolume edition has proven the great majority of the notebook entries, confirming both their depth and, in a minority of cases, their error.

research.topic: The afterlife of the mock theta functions and the lost notebook
research.content: The 'lost notebook,' rediscovered by George Andrews in 1976, continues to generate new mathematics. The mock theta functions, defined by Ramanujan in his final 1920 letter without a general theory, were embedded by Sander Zwegers in the early 2000s within the framework of harmonic Maass forms, and have since been connected to areas including partition theory and mathematical physics. This is an active research area, and the full reach of Ramanujan's late work is still being mapped.

research.topic: The cause of Ramanujan's death and the workings of his intuition
research.content: What evidence would resolve the cause of death: contemporary clinical records, laboratory results, or a documented autopsy. Why unavailable: the surviving medical record from 1919–1920 is sparse and was made under wartime and colonial conditions; no conclusive diagnostic evidence survives. What can be established: he was gravely ill in England, returned to India in 1919, and died in 1920; tuberculosis was the long-standing attribution, with a later reassessment favoring hepatic amoebiasis. What cannot be established: a certain cause of death, or a full account of the cognitive process by which he found his results, since he rarely recorded his reasoning.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Untaught Genius
comparativeNarrative.content: Within mathematics, Ramanujan is treated as a singular natural event. He reached results of great depth with almost none of the usual training. The interest is partly in the results, which are still used, and partly in the puzzle of how he found them. Hardy set the tone by saying he had never met anyone like him. Later mathematicians shifted the focus. Bruce Berndt and George Andrews moved it away from the romance of pure intuition. They turned instead to the slow labor of proving what he had simply asserted. From this angle the central fact is the mathematics. The life matters mainly as the unlikely soil it grew from.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Talent the Empire Almost Missed
comparativeNarrative.content: Seen from the history of colonial India, the story is about a vast gulf of money, race, and distance. Talent had to cross that gulf to be recognized at all. A poor clerk in a Madras shipping office had no obvious path to Cambridge. The imperial system both blocked his passage and, through its own institutions, enabled it. His honors in Britain were real. They also fit a comfortable imperial story about the empire discovering native ability. For many Indians he became a source of pride. He was proof that the subcontinent produced minds the equal of any in Britain. The hard question this view presses is how many similar talents the same system simply never reached.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: Religion and the self-understanding of genius
comparativeNarrative.name: The Mathematician Who Credited a Goddess
comparativeNarrative.content: A devout Hindu, Ramanujan said his formulas came from the goddess Namagiri of Namakkal, shown to him in dreams. For a view attentive to religion, this is not a detail to explain away. It is a central fact about how he experienced his own gift. It places him in a long line of people who saw insight as something received, not constructed. The disagreement here is about meaning, not fact. Some read the goddess as a sincere way of describing a natural gift. Others take the religious experience seriously on its own terms. Either way, the usual account treats the goddess as charming background. That risks missing how central she was to the man.

comparativeSummary: Three vantages on Ramanujan that emphasize different cores. The history of mathematics fixes on the results and the puzzle of a mind that found them without the usual training. Colonial and Indian history reads the same life as a story about distance and recognition, about the gulf a poor clerk had to cross and the talents the imperial structure never reached. A religious perspective takes seriously his own account, that the goddess Namagiri gave him the formulas in dreams. The disagreement is less about facts than about what counts as the center of the story: the mathematics, the colonial passage, or the way one of the modern world's great minds understood his own gift.

rabbitHole.label: Al-Khwarizmi
rabbitHole.reason: A foundational mathematician of an earlier age, whose name gave us 'algebra' and 'algorithm.' Setting Ramanujan beside him offers a long-view comparison of what it means to leave a permanent mark on the discipline, across very different worlds and a thousand years.

rabbitHole.label: Alan Turing
rabbitHole.reason: Another singular, partly tragic mathematical mind of the same century who worked in Britain and died young. The two make a natural pair: different countries of origin, different fields, a shared pattern of extraordinary originality cut short.

rabbitHole.label: Colonialism
rabbitHole.reason: The gulf Ramanujan crossed from Madras to Cambridge was a colonial one, of money, race, and distance. His story shows how the colonial relationship shaped whose talent got seen, on what terms, and at what cost to the person who crossed it.

rabbitHole.label: The British Empire
rabbitHole.reason: The imperial framework both blocked Ramanujan's path and, through Cambridge and the Royal Society, enabled it. His recognition was genuine and also legible within an imperial narrative about the empire elevating native talent, a tension the empire's structure produced.

rabbitHole.label: Hinduism
rabbitHole.reason: Ramanujan was a devout Hindu who credited his formulas to the goddess Namagiri, revealed in dreams. His faith was the frame through which he understood his own gift, making the religious tradition inseparable from the mathematics he produced.

rabbitHole.label: Einstein
rabbitHole.reason: A near-contemporary by-word for revolutionary genius. Comparing the two clarifies how 'genius' is recognized and mythologized, and how differently the same century treated a celebrated European physicist and a self-taught Indian mathematician.

rabbitHole.label: Calculus
rabbitHole.reason: Ramanujan's work on infinite series, continued fractions, and asymptotic formulas sits squarely within the analytic tradition that calculus made possible. The tools of the calculus are the ground on which much of his number theory and analysis stands.

reference.author: Kanigel, Robert
reference.title: The Man Who Knew Infinity: A Life of the Genius Ramanujan
reference.annotation: The standard modern biography and the source of much of the popular framing of Ramanujan's life. Kanigel balances the human story, the colonial setting, and the mathematics, and gives the fullest accessible account of the journey from Kumbakonam to Cambridge. The natural starting point for the life rather than the technical work.

reference.author: Hardy, G. H.
reference.title: Ramanujan: Twelve Lectures on Subjects Suggested by His Life and Work
reference.annotation: Hardy's own account of Ramanujan's mathematics and character, written by the collaborator who discovered him. It is both a primary source on the partnership and a foundational technical survey, though it carries Hardy's particular framing of his colleague's strangeness and originality.

reference.author: Berndt, Bruce C.
reference.title: Ramanujan's Notebooks (Parts I–V)
reference.annotation: The definitive editorial project, supplying proofs and commentary for the thousands of results Ramanujan recorded without demonstration. Berndt's volumes are where the claim that his leaps were partly reconstructible is actually substantiated, and the indispensable reference for anyone engaging the mathematics seriously.

reference.author: Andrews, George E.; Berndt, Bruce C.
reference.title: Ramanujan's Lost Notebook (Parts I–V)
reference.annotation: The scholarly edition of the manuscript Andrews rediscovered in 1976, including the mock theta functions and much of Ramanujan's late work. Establishes the basis for the claim that his final mathematics continues to generate new results, and documents the connection to modern theory.

reference.author: Hardy, G. H.
reference.title: A Mathematician's Apology
reference.annotation: Hardy's reflective essay on the nature and value of pure mathematics, written late in his life. It contains his own assessment of the Ramanujan collaboration, including the remark that discovering Ramanujan was his greatest contribution, and is useful for understanding the vantage from which Hardy framed him.

commerce.title: The Man Who Knew Infinity: A Life of the Genius Ramanujan
commerce.author: Robert Kanigel
commerce.note: The first book to read if you have never encountered Ramanujan. Kanigel tells the whole human story, from a poor South Indian town to Cambridge and back, without assuming any mathematics. The book that makes most readers fall in love with the subject.

commerce.title: Ramanujan: Twelve Lectures on Subjects Suggested by His Life and Work
commerce.author: G. H. Hardy
commerce.note: Reach for this once the life makes sense and you want the mathematics from the man who knew it best. Hardy assumes some mathematical background, but conveys what was remarkable about the work better than any secondary account.

commerce.title: My Search for Ramanujan: How I Learned to Count
commerce.author: Ken Ono and Amir D. Aczel
commerce.note: Good for a reader who wants the mathematics carried by a personal narrative. A leading number theorist interleaves Ramanujan's legacy with his own life, showing how the work still shapes living research.

commerce.title: Ramanujan's Notebooks (Parts I–V)
commerce.author: Bruce C. Berndt
commerce.note: For the reader who wants the actual mathematics, proven and annotated. Demanding and technical, this is the definitive edition of the notebooks and the place where Ramanujan's results are made rigorous.

commerce.title: Ramanujan's Lost Notebook (Parts I–V)
commerce.author: George E. Andrews and Bruce C. Berndt
commerce.note: Turn to this for the late work, including the mock theta functions, in full scholarly form. The reference for understanding why the 'lost notebook' still drives current research.

popularCulture.title: The Man Who Knew Infinity (2015)
popularCulture.description: Dev Patel plays Ramanujan and Jeremy Irons plays Hardy in this film of Kanigel's biography. It carries the human story to a wide audience: the poverty, the goddess, the cold of England, the bond with Hardy. It smooths the timeline and tidies the mathematics into a few clean moments, and it leans on the clash between intuition and proof more neatly than life allowed.

popularCulture.title: The Man Who Knew Infinity (biography)
popularCulture.description: Robert Kanigel's 1991 book is how most people first meet Ramanujan. It made him a recognizable figure beyond mathematics and shaped the popular image of the self-taught genius. The story is true to the record, but its warmth can make a strange, uneven mind feel more like a tidy legend than he was.

popularCulture.title: The number 1729
popularCulture.description: The 'taxicab number' is Ramanujan's most famous story. Told that the cab number 1729 was dull, he said it was the smallest number that is a sum of two cubes in two ways. The tale spread far beyond math, standing in for his strange closeness to numbers. It also flattens a deep mind into a single party trick.

popularCulture.title: Namagiri and the dream formulas
popularCulture.description: Ramanujan said the goddess Namagiri showed him formulas in his sleep. The image is everywhere in how he is remembered: genius as something received, not worked out. It captures his real faith and how creation felt to him. But repeated as a charming legend, it can hide the long hours of effort his notebooks actually record.

popularCulture.title: National Mathematics Day in India
popularCulture.description: India marks Ramanujan's birthday, December 22, as National Mathematics Day. Schools hold events in his name, and he stands as a national symbol of homegrown genius. The honor is real and earned. It can also turn a complicated, sickly, isolated man into a smooth icon, and quietly skip past the colonial gulf his life had to cross.
